verses.text: And Elijah the Tishbite, who was of the inhabitants of Gilead, said unto Ahab, As the LORD God of Israel liveth, before whom I stand, there shall not be dew nor rain these years, but according to my word. Guidance: Elijah's arrival — one of the most dramatic entrances in the Old Testament. The narrator gives us no backstory, no genealogy beyond a single town: 'Elijah the Tishbite, who was of the inhabitants of Gilead.' Tishbe is a small Gileadite village; Gilead is the rugged Transjordan region. Elijah comes from the rough country, from outside the Samaria royal-court establishment, with no court connections, no priestly pedigree, no prior introduction. He simply appears. The name itself is the chapter's first declaration: Eliyahu — 'My God is YHWH.' In a kingdom where Ahab has just imported Baal-worship and built a Baal-temple in Samaria, a man named 'My God is the LORD' walks into the royal presence. The encounter: Elijah stands before Ahab and declares a drought. The oath formula is striking: 'As the LORD God of Israel liveth, before whom I stand.' (1) 'The LORD God of Israel liveth' — a deliberate rebuke. Baal was the storm-god, supposedly the giver of rain. Ahab's kingdom is now in covenant with a god who fertilizes the land through Baal's worship. Elijah's oath asserts that the LORD is the living God, regardless of which deity the royal court formally honors. (2) 'Before whom I stand' (asher amadti lefanav) — the standard idiom for being in a king's service. Elijah is implicitly claiming his true allegiance: he stands before the LORD as a courtier stands before a king. Ahab may be the temporal ruler; the LORD is Elijah's actual lord. (3) The content: 'There shall not be dew nor rain these years, but according to my word.' The dryness will not be brief — Hebrew shanim — years. (James 5:17 specifies three and a half years.) The prophet claims that not even the morning dew will come except by his word. The chapter is establishing the theological contest. Baal is the rain-god in Phoenician theology; the very thing Baal exists to deliver, the LORD will withhold, through Elijah's word. The drought that will dominate the next three years is a precisely-targeted theological challenge: the deity who supposedly controls weather will be publicly shown unable to deliver weather, while the LORD whom Ahab has displaced demonstrates absolute control. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's confrontations with false worship are not abstract debates — they unfold in the physical realities (rain, food, life, death) the false gods were supposed to control. Elijah's arrival opens the longest sustained prophetic narrative in the Old Testament. The next chapters will unfold one of the most concentrated demonstrations of divine power in Scripture, all initiated by this single sentence.

And the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, Get thee hence, and turn thee eastward, and hide thyself by the brook Cherith, that is before Jordan. And it shall be, that thou shalt drink of the brook; and I have commanded the ravens to feed thee there. So he went and did according unto the word of the LORD: for he went and dwelt by the brook Cherith, that is before Jordan. And the ravens brought him bread and flesh in the morning, and bread and flesh in the evening; and he drank of the brook. Guidance: The Cherith retreat. After the public confrontation comes the hidden sustenance. Elijah's drought-declaration is a political act of war on Ahab's regime; the king will certainly try to kill the prophet who spoke it. The LORD therefore sends Elijah into hiding. (1) The location. 'The brook Cherith, that is before Jordan.' Cherith — possibly the modern Wadi Qelt or a similar Transjordanian ravine — was a seasonal stream feeding the Jordan from the east. The geography is significant: Elijah retreats back to his native Transjordan, hidden in a wadi that even most Israelites would not know how to find. The LORD has prepared a hiding place beyond Ahab's reach. (2) The provision: water from the brook, bread and flesh from the ravens. Hebrew orevim — ravens. The same large black scavenger-birds that the Mosaic law had labeled unclean (Lev 11:15). Ravens normally hoard their food for themselves; they do not regularly carry food in their beaks to deliver to humans. The LORD has reversed the bird's nature for this specific service. 'In the morning, and in the evening' — the same morning-and-evening rhythm of the temple sacrifices. The LORD is providing Elijah meals on the priestly schedule, far from any temple. (3) The implicit lesson. The LORD who has just shut off the rain still feeds His prophet. The same God who controls drought controls also the corner of His hiding-place that overflows. The chapter is showing us a deep theological principle: divine judgment on a nation does not mean divine abandonment of His servants in that nation. The same drought that wastes Israel's grain leaves bread arriving twice daily by raven-courier at the wadi. (4) The Mosaic-law detail. The ravens are technically unclean birds. The LORD is willing to use any creature to provide for His prophet, ritual-cleanliness categories notwithstanding. Christians have read this as an early hint of God's willingness to set aside ritual barriers when His mercy requires it — the same principle Peter would face on the rooftop in Joppa (Acts 10). The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's provision often comes through means we would not have chosen and might have ritually rejected. The widow of Zarephath in the next paragraph will continue the same pattern; she is a foreigner, a Sidonian, technically outside the covenant community, but the LORD uses her to keep Elijah alive.

And it came to pass after a while, that the brook dried up, because there had been no rain in the land. Guidance: The brook dries. The drought Elijah declared affected even the brook Elijah depended on. The LORD did not exempt the prophet from the consequences of his own prophecy. Hebrew miqtzeh yamim — at the end of days, after a while. The brook had run for some time; now it is gone. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's servants are not exempt from hard providences. Elijah did not get a private oasis while the rest of Israel suffered. The drought he prophesied came also to the wadi where he hid. The chapter is teaching us a sobering principle: even those carrying out the LORD's purposes will face the consequences of those purposes. The minister who preaches hard truths to his people will share their suffering when the truths land. The prophet who announces drought drinks dried-up brook-water. The chapter is also setting up the next stage. Why did the LORD let the brook dry up before moving Elijah? Because the prophet's continued safety required relocation. The LORD's plan for Elijah unfolded in stages: first Cherith, then Zarephath. The drying brook is the LORD's way of moving him from the first stage to the second. Providence often communicates through circumstance: when the resources at a location dry up, it may be the LORD's signal to move.

And the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, Arise, get thee to Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon, and dwell there: behold, I have commanded a widow woman there to sustain thee. Guidance: The relocation to Zarephath. The LORD sends Elijah to 'Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon.' Zarephath (modern Sarafand in Lebanon) was a coastal town in Phoenicia — Jezebel's own homeland. The location is striking. The man who had prophesied against Ahab's Phoenician-influenced kingdom is now sent to live in Phoenicia itself. Jezebel cannot find him in her own backyard. The LORD's hiding places can be the very places His enemies would think to look last. The provision: 'I have commanded a widow woman there to sustain thee.' Hebrew tzivvitti — I have commanded. Just as He commanded the ravens, the LORD has now commanded a widow. The detail is telling — the LORD's command operates not just on birds but on human hearts; He has prepared a specific woman in Zarephath whose readiness to receive Elijah is already accomplished. The chapter is showing us another instance of unlikely provision. A widow in famine-stricken Phoenicia — exactly the most economically vulnerable kind of person in the ancient world — has been designated to feed the LORD's prophet. The provision the LORD has prepared is not from the powerful but from the marginal. Jesus would later note this episode specifically: 'Many widows were in Israel in the days of Elias, when the heaven was shut up three years and six months, when great famine was throughout all the land; But unto none of them was Elias sent, save unto Sarepta, a city of Sidon, unto a woman that was a widow' (Luke 4:25-26). Jesus' point: the LORD's mercy crosses ethnic boundaries when His own people refuse Him. The widow of Sidon receives what Israelite widows did not, because she was prepared to receive. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's provision often comes through unexpected channels and to unexpected recipients — and that those who reject Him within the covenant community may find Him going outside the community to find the heart that will welcome Him.

And the word of the LORD came unto him, saying, Arise, get thee to Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon, and dwell there: behold, I have commanded a widow woman there to sustain thee. Guidance: The relocation to Zarephath. The LORD sends Elijah to 'Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon.' Zarephath (modern Sarafand in Lebanon) was a coastal town in Phoenicia — Jezebel's own homeland. So he arose and went to Zarephath. And when he came to the gate of the city, behold, the widow woman was there gathering of sticks: and he called to her, and said, Fetch me, I pray thee, a little water in a vessel, that I may drink. And as she was going to fetch it, he called to her, and said, Bring me, I pray thee, a morsel of bread in thine hand. And she said, As the LORD thy God liveth, I have not a cake, but an handful of meal in a barrel, and a little oil in a cruse: and, behold, I am gathering two sticks, that I may go in and dress it for me and my son, that we may eat it, and die. Guidance: The encounter at the gate. Elijah arrives in Zarephath. At the city gate (the standard gathering place for the town's commerce, judgment, and social life), he sees a widow gathering sticks — collecting fuel for what she expects to be her last meal. Elijah's request comes in two stages. (1) Water. 'Fetch me, I pray thee, a little water in a vessel, that I may drink.' She begins to comply — she does not refuse. (2) Bread. As she walks away, he adds: 'Bring me, I pray thee, a morsel of bread in thine hand.' Her response is heartbreaking. 'As the LORD thy God liveth' — the widow uses the covenant name of the God of Israel. (Her oath formula 'thy God' — not 'my God' — recognizes Elijah's identity without claiming it for herself.) 'I have not a cake, but an handful of meal in a barrel, and a little oil in a cruse.' The Hebrew phrasing is deliberate — kaph qemach (a palm of flour) and me'at shemen (a little oil). She has nothing but what fits in one cupped hand. 'I am gathering two sticks, that I may go in and dress it for me and my son, that we may eat it, and die.' She is preparing the family's last meal. She has counted her resources and concluded: this is the end. Her stick-gathering at the city gate is not provident preparation; it is the final act of a woman whose flour and oil are about to give out. The chapter is showing us the depth of the famine in Phoenicia. Even outside Israel, even in the cosmopolitan trading-cities of the coast, the drought had reduced families to single-meal survival. And yet the LORD has chosen this household as His provision-source. He sends Elijah to a woman who has less than nothing for herself, much less for a hungry stranger. The chapter is preparing us for the test that follows. Elijah will ask her to give him the bread first — to feed the stranger before feeding her own dying child. The request will be the moment of the widow's faith.

And Elijah said unto her, Fear not; go and do as thou hast said: but make me thereof a little cake first, and bring it unto me, and after make for thee and for thy son. For thus saith the LORD God of Israel, The barrel of meal shall not waste, neither shall the cruse of oil fail, until the day that the LORD sendeth rain upon the earth. And she went and did according to the saying of Elijah: and she, and he, and her house, did eat many days. And the barrel of meal wasted not, neither did the cruse of oil fail, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by Elijah. Guidance: The miracle of the never-empty jar. Elijah's response begins with 'Fear not' (al-tira'i) — the standard biblical-encounter opener that often signals divine intervention. 'Go and do as thou hast said: but make me thereof a little cake first.' He acknowledges her plan (make the last meal) but inserts a single instruction — make a small cake for him first. This is the test. To bake him bread first, with one cupped hand of flour, would necessarily reduce what was left for her son. The widow must trust that the stranger's promise will hold. The promise: 'Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, The barrel of meal shall not waste, neither shall the cruse of oil fail, until the day that the LORD sendeth rain upon the earth.' Hebrew kad ha-qemach lo tikhleh ve-tzapachat ha-shemen lo techsar. The verbs are precise — 'shall not be finished' and 'shall not lack.' The barrel and cruse will perpetually have enough, however much is taken out, until the drought ends. The widow goes and does it. She bakes the small cake for Elijah first; then more cake for herself and her son. 'She, and he, and her house, did eat many days.' Hebrew yamim — for an extended period, multiple years (the chapter's narrative span will cover the rest of the drought). The chapter is recording one of the most beautiful miracle-stories in the Old Testament. Every day for years, the widow reached into the barrel and there was flour; every day, the cruse poured and there was oil. The supply was always small (just enough for the day) but never empty (always something for the day). The principle: just-enough provision sustained through impossible circumstances. The chapter is teaching us a profound lesson about faith and provision. The widow's faith was not believing she had abundance; it was believing the LORD would provide tomorrow when today she had only enough for today. She trusted Elijah's word against the evidence of her own dwindling jar. She gave him the first cake when she had every economic reason to keep it. The result: the jar never emptied. The chapter is showing us a pattern: when we give the LORD the first portion of what little we have, the rest does not run out. The widow's mite (Mark 12) is the same principle in the Gospels. The first-fruits offering (Lev 23) is the same principle in the Law. The Lord's prayer's request for 'daily bread' is the same expectation. The LORD does not always provide abundance; He often provides exactly enough, day by day, sustained by His ongoing word.

And Elijah said unto her, Fear not; go and do as thou hast said: but make me thereof a little cake first, and bring it unto me, and after make for thee and for thy son. For thus saith the LORD God of Israel, The barrel of meal shall not waste, neither shall the cruse of oil fail, until the day that the LORD sendeth rain upon the earth. And she went and did according to the saying of Elijah: and she, and he, and her house, did eat many days. And the barrel of meal wasted not, neither did the cruse of oil fail, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by Elijah. Guidance: The miracle of the never-empty jar. Elijah's response begins with 'Fear not' (al-tira'i) — the standard biblical-encounter opener that often signals divine intervention. 'Go and do as thou hast said: but make me thereof a little cake first.' He acknowledges her plan (make the last meal) but inserts a single instruction — make a small cake for him first. This is the test. To bake him bread first, with one cupped hand of flour, would necessarily reduce what was left for her son. The widow must trust that the stranger's promise will hold. The promise: 'Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, The barrel of meal shall not waste, neither shall the cruse of oil fail, until the day that the LORD sendeth rain upon the earth.' Hebrew kad ha-qemach lo tikhleh ve-tzapachat ha-shemen lo techsar. The verbs are precise — 'shall not be finished' and 'shall not lack.' The barrel and cruse will perpetually have enough, however much is taken out, until the drought ends. The widow goes and does it. She bakes the small cake for Elijah first; then more cake for herself and her son. 'She, and he, and her house, did eat many days.' Hebrew yamim — for an extended period, multiple years (the chapter's narrative span will cover the rest of the drought). The chapter is recording one of the most beautiful miracle-stories in the Old Testament. Every day for years, the widow reached into the barrel and there was flour; every day, the cruse poured and there was oil. The supply was always small (just enough for the day) but never empty (always something for the day). The principle: just-enough provision sustained through impossible circumstances. The chapter is teaching us a profound lesson about faith and provision. The widow's faith was not believing she had abundance; it was believing the LORD would provide tomorrow when today she had only enough for today. She trusted Elijah's word against the evidence of her own dwindling jar. She gave him the first cake when she had every economic reason to keep it. The result: the jar never emptied. The chapter is showing us a pattern: when we give the LORD the first portion of what little we have, the rest does not run out. The widow's mite (Mark 12) is the same principle in the Gospels. The first-fruits offering (Lev 23) is the same principle in the Law. The Lord's prayer's request for 'daily bread' is the same expectation. The LORD does not always provide abundance; He often provides exactly enough, day by day, sustained by His ongoing word.

And it came to pass after these things, that the son of the woman, the mistress of the house, fell sick; and his sickness was so sore, that there was no breath left in him. And she said unto Elijah, What have I to do with thee, O thou man of God? art thou come unto me to call my sin to remembrance, and to slay my son? Guidance: The son's death and the widow's anguish. The miracle of the jar continued for months, perhaps years. Then a new crisis: the widow's son falls sick, and the illness escalates until 'there was no breath left in him' (lo notrah-bo neshamah — no breath remaining in him). The son for whom she had refused to make the last meal earlier in the chapter, the son the LORD's jar had been feeding all this time, is now dead. The widow's response is raw. 'What have I to do with thee, O thou man of God?' Hebrew mah-li va-lakh — 'what to me and to you?' — the standard idiom for 'why have you intruded into my life?' (compare Jesus to His mother at Cana, John 2:4). 'Art thou come unto me to call my sin to remembrance, and to slay my son?' The widow interprets her child's death as divine punishment for some sin in her past — perhaps long-buried, perhaps simply known to her conscience but otherwise ignored. The arrival of the holy stranger has, in her mind, brought her sin under divine scrutiny; the death is the consequence. Theologically, she is not entirely wrong about the principle (the LORD does sometimes use circumstances to confront sin), but she is wrong about the application (this death will be the occasion of her deliverance, not her punishment). The chapter is preserving an honest moment. The widow does not respond with measured theology; she responds with grief-driven accusation. The LORD's miracles in her life do not exempt her from devastating loss. The jar of meal had not failed for months — but her son still died. The chapter is showing us that small daily mercies do not always prevent large life-shaking losses. The widow had been miraculously fed for months; her child died anyway. The chapter is teaching us that God's care is not the absence of suffering but the presence-through-suffering. The widow is about to find that the same God who has been keeping her flour also has power over death itself.

And he said unto her, Give me thy son. And he took him out of her bosom, and carried him up into a loft, where he abode, and laid him upon his own bed. And he cried unto the LORD, and said, O LORD my God, hast thou also brought evil upon the widow with whom I sojourn, by slaying her son? And he stretched himself upon the child three times, and cried unto the LORD, and said, O LORD my God, I pray thee, let this child's soul come into him again. Guidance: Elijah's intercession. He responds to the widow's accusation not with theological argument but with action. 'Give me thy son.' He takes the dead child out of his mother's arms (the chapter's heartbreaking detail: 'out of her bosom' — me-cheiqah — from her embrace; she was still holding him), carries him up to the upper-room (aliyyah — the prophet's lodging on the upper floor), and lays him on his own bed. Then comes the intercession. Three elements. (1) The complaint to the LORD. 'O LORD my God, hast thou also brought evil upon the widow with whom I sojourn, by slaying her son?' Note 'also' (gam) — Elijah implies that the widow has already been afflicted enough by the drought; now the LORD has added her son's death. The prophet is not afraid to question the LORD's apparent harshness. The boldness of the address is striking; Elijah does not pretend the situation is acceptable. He brings his bewilderment to God honestly. (2) The physical action. 'He stretched himself upon the child three times.' Hebrew yithoded — he stretched himself out, lay full-length, on the child. The bodily contact is symbolic of the prophet's complete identification with the child's plight. The three repetitions may reflect the threefold completeness common in biblical numerology. (3) The petition. 'O LORD my God, I pray thee, let this child's soul come into him again.' Hebrew tashav na nephesh-ha-yeled ha-zeh al-qirbo — 'let the soul/breath of this child return into him.' This is the first recorded resurrection prayer in the Old Testament. The Hebrew nephesh — soul, life-breath — is the same word for the breath that left the child in v17. The prophet asks for the breath to return. The chapter is showing us prophetic intercession at its most intense. Elijah does not stand aloof; he physically engages, vocally pleads, and identifies himself with the grief. The chapter is teaching us that intercession is not detached prayer but embodied advocacy. Elijah brings his entire being to bear: his body stretched on the child, his voice raised to God, his honest complaint mixed with his urgent request. The chapter is preserving a model for how to pray when faced with apparently irreversible catastrophe — bring it all to God, honestly, fully, and ask Him to do the impossible.

And the LORD heard the voice of Elijah; and the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived. And Elijah took the child, and brought him down out of the chamber into the house, and delivered him unto his mother: and Elijah said, See, thy son liveth. Guidance: The revival. 'The LORD heard the voice of Elijah; and the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived.' Hebrew vayyishma YHWH be-qol Eliyahu va-tashav nephesh ha-yeled al-qirbo va-yechi. Three precise verbs: the LORD heard, the soul returned, the child lived. The first recorded resurrection in the canonical Old Testament. (Earlier resurrections in Genesis are not explicit; this is the first clear case of a dead person restored to life.) Elijah carries the living child back down the stairs to his mother. 'See, thy son liveth.' Hebrew re'i chai bnekh — 'look, your son lives.' The phrase is bare; no elaborate explanation, no theological lecture. The mother holds her son again; the prophet steps back; the LORD has done what the LORD does. The chapter is showing us the LORD's power extending to the absolute boundary — death itself. The drought-controller, the food-multiplier, is also the life-giver. There is no domain over which His power does not extend. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's word is more than sustaining; it is restoring. Where the jar of meal had sustained life, the resurrection now restores life. The LORD's care moves through layers: from bread to breath, from daily food to undying life. The chapter is offering a pre-Christian glimpse of resurrection theology. The Christian gospel will eventually announce a Resurrection that does not return one boy to mortal life but raises Christ to immortal life — and through Him, raises all His people. The widow's son is a foreshadowing; he will eventually die again (presumably in old age). But the principle is established: the LORD has power over death. That principle, once introduced, will not leave the biblical narrative until it culminates in the empty tomb.

And the LORD heard the voice of Elijah; and the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived. And the woman said to Elijah, Now by this I know that thou art a man of God, and that the word of the LORD in thy mouth is truth. Guidance: The widow's confession. 'Now by this I know that thou art a man of God, and that the word of the LORD in thy mouth is truth.' Hebrew attah zeh yadati ki ish Elohim attah u-devar-YHWH be-fikha emet. Three affirmations. (1) 'I know' (yadati) — the verb of certain knowledge. She had previously trusted Elijah enough to bake him a cake; she had previously called the LORD 'thy God.' Now her knowledge has deepened to personal conviction. (2) 'Thou art a man of God' (ish Elohim attah). The full prophetic title. The widow recognizes that Elijah's authority is not personal but divine — he is a vessel through which God works. (3) 'The word of the LORD in thy mouth is truth' (devar-YHWH be-fikha emet). The widow's faith is now not merely in Elijah but in the LORD's word as spoken through Elijah. The chapter is closing on the transformation of the widow. She began as a stranger preparing her last meal; she ends as a believer in the LORD God of Israel. Through her drought-stricken poverty, the LORD has brought her to faith. Through the death and resurrection of her son, He has confirmed it. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD often uses the deepest sufferings to produce the deepest faith. The widow's miracle-fed flour-jar had sustained her body but had not, apparently, produced this level of theological confession. The death of her son and the prayer that raised him did. The greater the crisis, the greater the revelation. The chapter is also preparing us for what is coming. The widow's confession is a foretaste of what the LORD intends to produce in all Israel through Elijah — public, certain, personal recognition that the LORD God of Israel is the true God and that His word in His prophet's mouth is truth. The next chapter will move that demonstration from a private widow's house to the public arena of Mount Carmel. The chapter is ending with a confession in Phoenician territory; the next chapter will produce a chorus of confession on Israelite soil. The Elijah cycle has begun. Everything from here forward will be one of the most concentrated demonstrations of the LORD's power in the Old Testament.
